abstract: In Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, action chunking (i.e., executing a sequence of actions without intermediate replanning) is a key technique to improve robotic manipulation abilities. However, a large chunk size reduces the model's responsiveness to new information, while a small one increases the likelihood of mode-jumping, jerky behavior resulting from discontinuities between chunks. Therefore, selecting the optimal chunk size is an urgent demand to balance the model's reactivity and consistency. Unfortunately, a dominant trend in current VLA models is an empirical fixed chunk length at inference-time, hindering their superiority and scalability across diverse manipulation tasks. To address this issue, we propose a novel Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) strategy, which exploits action entropy as the cue to adaptively determine the chunk size based on current predictions. Extensive experiments on a wide range of simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks have demonstrated that our approach substantially improves performance over the state-of-the-art alternatives. The videos and source code are publicly available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Johan Bjorck, Fernando Casta˜neda, Nikita Cherniadev, Xingye Da, Runyu Ding, Linxi Fan, Yu Fang, Dieter Fox, Fengyuan Hu, Spencer Huang, et al. GR00T N1: An open foundation model for generalist humanoid robots. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.14734, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces GR00T N1.5, which serves as the fundamental Vision-Language-Action (VLA) baseline model for the main paper. The proposed Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) method is implemented on top of and evaluated against this model, making it crucial for understanding the context and impact of the work.
citations.title: GR00T N1: An open foundation model for generalist humanoid robots

citations.fullCitation: Cheng Chi, Zhenjia Xu, Siyuan Feng, Eric Cousineau, Yilun Du, Benjamin Burchfiel, Russ Tedrake, and Shuran Song. Diffusion policy: Visuomotor policy learning via action diffusion. The International Journal of Robotics Research, 44: 1684–1704, 2023.
citations.justification: This is a foundational paper cited for popularizing action chunking with diffusion models in robotics. The main paper's core contribution is an improvement upon this action chunking paradigm, making this citation essential for understanding the underlying technique being adapted.
citations.title: Diffusion policy: Visuomotor policy learning via action diffusion

citations.fullCitation: Tony Zhao, Vikash Kumar, Sergey Levine, and Chelsea Finn. Learning fine-grained bimanual manipulation with low-cost hardware. Robotics: Science and Systems XIX, 2023.
citations.justification: This paper, known as ACT, is cited as an early and important study demonstrating the benefits of action chunking for imitation learning. The main paper contrasts its adaptive chunking method with ACT's approach of using Exponential Moving Average (EMA) to enhance temporal consistency, highlighting it as a key predecessor.
citations.title: Learning fine-grained bimanual manipulation with low-cost hardware

citations.fullCitation: Yuanwei Liu, Junwei Han, Xiwen Yao, Salman Khan, Hisham Cholakkal, Rao Muhammad Anwer, Nian Liu, and Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Bidirectional reciprocative information communication for few-shot semantic segmentation. In Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduces BID, a method that addresses the same problem of balancing a policy's consistency and reactivity. BID selects the optimal full action chunk from multiple candidates, providing a direct point of comparison to the main paper's strategy of adaptively selecting a partial chunk size.
citations.title: Bidirectional reciprocative information communication for few-shot semantic segmentation

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Adaptive Action Chunking at Inference-time for Vision-Language-Action Models."

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this paper are:
*   Yuanchang Liang (National University of Singapore)
*   Xiaobo Wang (Shenzhen University of Advanced Technology, Sangfor Technologies Inc.)
*   Kai Wang (National University of Singapore)
*   Shuo Wang (Mininglamp Technology)
*   Xiaojiang Peng (Shenzhen Technology University)
*   Haoyu Chen (City University of Hong Kong)
*   David Kim Huat Chua (National University of Singapore)
*   Prahlad Vadakkepat (National University of Singapore)

### 2. How this Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models represent a significant area of research in robotics, aiming to enable robots to generate control actions from diverse data modalities, including vision, language instructions, and proprioceptive states. These models often leverage techniques from large pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) and action experts, with diffusion or flow-matching mechanisms commonly employed for continuous action prediction. Recent VLA models, such as GR00T N1.5 and Diffusion Policy, have adopted "action chunking" as a standard paradigm. Action chunking involves executing a sequence of actions without intermediate replanning, which has been shown to reduce compounding errors and improve success rates in robotic manipulation tasks.

However, a prevalent challenge within this landscape is the empirical selection of a fixed action chunk size at inference time. This approach presents a dilemma: a large chunk size can lead to reduced reactivity to new environmental information, while a small chunk size may increase the likelihood of "mode-jumping" or jerky behavior due to discontinuities between chunks, and reduce computational efficiency. The optimal chunk size is often task-dependent and can vary even within different phases of a single task, making empirical tuning difficult and often suboptimal. Existing attempts to address this issue have included methods that integrate current and past actions using Exponential Moving Average (EMA) or employ search strategies based on coherence and contrast scores. More recent work has explored reinforcement learning to adapt chunk sizes, but these methods often rely on handcrafted value functions that lack generalization or task-specific reward signals unavailable in real-world scenarios. This paper positions itself within this context by proposing an inference-time adaptation strategy that aims to dynamically determine the action chunk size, thereby addressing the limitations of fixed-length chunking and the practical constraints of prior adaptive methods.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop an effective strategy for adaptively determining the action chunk size at inference time for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. This objective is driven by several key motivations:

*   **Balancing Reactivity and Consistency**: Current VLA models frequently employ a fixed action chunk length during inference. This fixed size presents a trade-off: a large chunk size can enhance temporal consistency and computational efficiency by executing longer action sequences, but it diminishes the model's ability to react to new, unforeseen information or changes in the environment. Conversely, a small chunk size increases reactivity and allows for more frequent replanning, but it can lead to issues such as mode-jumping (discontinuities between actions), jerky movements, and reduced computational efficiency due to more frequent replanning cycles. The research aims to achieve an optimal balance between these two conflicting requirements.
*   **Addressing Suboptimal Fixed Chunk Sizes**: Empirical evidence, demonstrated by the authors' experiments with GR00T N1.5 on RoboCasa Kitchen tasks, shows that the optimal action chunk size varies significantly across different manipulation tasks. For example, some tasks may benefit from larger chunks for broad movements, while others require smaller chunks for fine-grained control. Relying on a single, fixed chunk size, often determined through extensive empirical tuning, is inherently suboptimal and hinders the scalability and superior performance of VLA models across diverse manipulation scenarios.
*   **Overcoming Limitations of Prior Adaptive Approaches**: Previous attempts to introduce adaptive action chunking have encountered challenges. Some methods, like those based on Exponential Moving Average or search strategies, still rely on a fixed overall chunk size, limiting true adaptability. Reinforcement learning-based approaches for dynamic chunk selection have been proposed, but these often depend on hand-crafted value functions that lack generalization or require task-specific reward signals (e.g., from simulation) which are not readily available in real-world deployments. The motivation is to provide a practical and generalized solution that operates solely at inference time without requiring additional training or architectural modifications.
*   **Improving Performance Across Diverse Tasks**: By dynamically adjusting the chunk size based on the model's predictive uncertainty, the goal is to enhance the success rates of robotic manipulation tasks. This is particularly relevant for complex, long-horizon tasks that involve various subtasks, where different phases (e.g., reaching, grasping, transporting, placing) may inherently demand different levels of control granularity.

In summary, the motivation is to move beyond the limitations of static, empirically-tuned action chunking in VLA models by introducing a dynamic, uncertainty-aware strategy that improves task success rates, adaptability, and operational safety, all while maintaining practical applicability in both simulated and real-world environments.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The proposed method, Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC), is an inference-time strategy designed to dynamically select the optimal action chunk size based on the model's predictive uncertainty. The approach explicitly avoids additional training or architectural modifications to the underlying VLA model.

**Baseline VLA Model:** The paper adopts GR00T N1.5 as the baseline VLA model for implementation. GR00T N1.5 is described as a foundation model with a flow-matching action head. It comprises a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for extracting vision-language features ($\phi_t$) and a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) module serving as the action head ($V_\theta$). This action head is trained to predict a sequence of future actions (an action chunk $A_t$) by approximating a flow vector field, minimizing a flow-matching loss. At inference time, action chunks are generated through K-step iterative denoising from random noise. The AAC algorithm is presented as being applicable to any VLA model utilizing a diffusion-based action head.

**Core Idea: Action Entropy as a Cue:** The central concept of AAC is to use action entropy as a metric to quantify the uncertainty level of predicted actions. The hypothesis is that a lower entropy signifies higher reliability in the predicted actions, suggesting that a longer action chunk can be executed with confidence. Conversely, high entropy indicates low-quality or uncertain predictions, warranting smaller chunk sizes and more frequent replanning to improve reactivity.

**Action Entropy Calculation:**
The action space for a robotic arm (e.g., a 7-DOF arm with a gripper) is typically composed of both continuous and discrete controls:
*   **Continuous Control:** For 3-DOF translation offsets ($\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$) and 3-DOF rotation offsets ($\Delta R_x, \Delta R_y, \Delta R_z$), the Gaussian Differential entropy is used. This requires estimating the covariance matrix ($\Sigma_t$) of the predicted continuous actions.
*   **Discrete Control:** For a 1-DOF gripper control (G $\in \{0, 1\}$), the standard discrete entropy formula is applied, based on the probability $p(a)$ of each action.

To estimate the probability $p(a)$ for discrete actions and the covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ for continuous actions, AAC samples *N* candidate action chunks in parallel. For instance, the probability of a gripper-close state is estimated as the count of close-state occurrences among the *N* samples, divided by *N*.

The average action entropy for a given chunk size *h* ($E_h$) is calculated by summing the individual entropies (translation, rotation, gripper) for each timestep within the chunk, then averaging over *h* timesteps.

**Optimal Chunk Size Determination:**
The optimal chunk size ($h^*$) is determined by finding the point where the average action entropy exhibits the maximum differential, implying a significant change in uncertainty. Specifically, $h^* = \text{max}(\text{arg max}_h(E_{h+1} - E_h), \xi)$.
*   The term $\text{arg max}_h(E_{h+1} - E_h)$ identifies the chunk size *h* where the increase in average entropy from *h* to *h+1* is maximal. This point is considered where the model's confidence starts to drop significantly.
*   $\xi$ represents a lower bound on the chunk size, ensuring a minimum action magnitude. This minimum magnitude is defined by summing component magnitudes for translation (Euclidean norm of vector from start to end of chunk), rotation (accumulated offsets via quaternion composition), and gripper motion (1 if state changes, 0 otherwise). This constraint helps maintain temporal consistency and computational efficiency, preventing overly small chunks that could lead to jerky behavior or excessive replanning.

**Inference-time Operation:**
The entire AAC algorithm operates as follows at each inference timestep:
1.  Generate *N* candidate action chunks in parallel using the VLA model.
2.  For each timestep within each candidate chunk, compute the action entropy for continuous (translation, rotation) and discrete (gripper) components.
3.  Calculate the average action entropy ($E_h$) for different potential chunk sizes *h* (from 1 up to the maximum horizon *H*).
4.  Determine the optimal chunk size $h^*$ using the maximum differential point of average action entropy, constrained by the minimum action magnitude $\xi$.
5.  Execute the first $h^*$ actions of the predicted action chunk.
6.  Replanning occurs at the next observation based on the newly executed $h^*$ actions.

This methodology allows AAC to dynamically adapt the chunk size during task execution, enabling larger chunks for confident, coarse movements (e.g., transportation) and smaller chunks for critical, fine-grained manipulations (e.g., grasping), thereby striking a balance between consistency and reactivity.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research paper presents extensive experimental validation across both simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) strategy.

**5.1. Simulation Experiments**

**Benchmarks:**
*   **RoboCasa Kitchen**: A benchmark of 24 kitchen manipulation tasks categorized into Relocation, Container, Rotation, and Button tasks.
*   **LIBERO**: A benchmark comprising four task suites (LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, LIBERO-Goal, LIBERO-Long), each with 10 tasks, evaluating spatial reasoning, object interaction, and long-horizon objectives.

**Quantitative Results:**
*   **Comparison with Vanilla GR00T (Default h=16)**: AAC consistently improved task success rates.
    *   On RoboCasa, AAC achieved an average success rate of 62.0%, a 2.3% gain over GR00T's 59.7%. Notable improvements included a 3.8% increase in Rotation tasks (from 57.6% to 61.4%), suggesting enhanced capability for fine-grained control.
    *   On LIBERO, AAC improved the average success rate by 0.9% (from 94.1% to 95.0%). A significant gain of 4% was observed in the LIBERO-Long suite, indicating AAC's effectiveness in complex, multi-subtask, long-horizon scenarios.
*   **Comparison with Fixed-size GR00T (Various h)**: Experiments with GR00T using fixed chunk sizes (h=2, 4, 8, 12) demonstrated that no single fixed size was optimal across all tasks or even task subsets. For example, LIBERO-Spatial performed best with h=4, while LIBERO-Goal achieved highest success with h=16. AAC consistently outperformed all fixed-size baselines on average, demonstrating its ability to eliminate the need for manual, task-specific chunk-size tuning.

**Qualitative Results:**
*   **Distribution of Chunk Size Decision**: Analysis of chunk size decisions in a representative LIBERO-Spatial task ("Pick up the black bowl next to the cookie box and place it on the plate") showed that AAC's dynamic chunk size selection aligns with semantic task phases. Larger chunk sizes were observed when the gripper was far from the object (transportation stage), enabling faster, coarser movements. Smaller chunk sizes were predicted when the gripper approached and manipulated the object (critical manipulation stage), allowing for precise, fine control and higher-frequency observations.

**Scalability and Efficiency:**
*   **Different Backbones**: AAC was successfully applied to the $\pi_{0.5}$ VLA backbone, showing generalization capabilities and further improving success rates (e.g., from 97.0% to 97.9% average on LIBERO).
*   **Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Scenarios**: In experiments on LIBERO-Pro with varying levels of position perturbation, AAC consistently enhanced robustness and success rates compared to baseline methods under OOD conditions (e.g., 3.9% for GR00T vs. 6.3% for GR00T+AAC, and 30.9% for $\pi_{0.5}$ vs. 34.8% for $\pi_{0.5}$+AAC on average across perturbation levels).
*   **Number of Samples for Entropy Estimation**: Varying the number of parallel samples (N) for action entropy estimation showed that increasing samples generally improved success rates. An increase from 1 to 20 samples improved success rate by 0.9% (from 94.1% to 95.0% on LIBERO). Beyond 20 samples, improvements were marginal.
*   **Computational Efficiency**: While requiring multiple candidate chunk generations, the computational cost of AAC was found to be negligible due to batched parallel computation. Using 20 samples introduced approximately a 20ms inference delay on a single A800 GPU, indicating that the overhead is manageable.

**5.2. Real-world Experiments**

**Setup:**
*   A Realman single-arm robot with a Mycobot gripper was used.
*   Visual observations from a wrist RGB view and a side RGB view were collected.
*   GR00T N1.5 was fine-tuned on 50 human-teleoperated demonstrations per task.

**Tasks:**
*   **Banana Pick-and-Place**: Picking a banana and placing it into a basket, both with random positions, requiring precise gripper orientation.
*   **Emergency Button Pressing (E-Button)**: Pressing an emergency button, requiring high localization accuracy, with some button positions unseen during training.
*   **Long-Horizon Toy Manipulation**: Picking a randomly placed toy, placing it in a drawer, and closing the drawer, demanding precision throughout the trajectory.

**Results:**
*   **Improved Real-world Task Success Rates**: AAC consistently outperformed the baseline GR00T across all three real-world tasks.
    *   Average success rate increased by 15% (from 67.0% to 82.0%).
    *   Specific gains: Banana pick-and-place (70.0% to 90.0%), E-Button pressing (65.0% to 75.0%), and Long-horizon toy manipulation (65.0% to 80.0%).
    *   The improvements were attributed to AAC's ability to facilitate better gripper alignment, generalization to unseen object locations, and enhanced precision in multi-stage tasks.
*   **Improved Action Accuracy and Safety**: Qualitative observations indicated that AAC prevented collisions with the tabletop by filtering high-entropy (uncertain) actions, allowing the robot to smoothly stop at appropriate points. This demonstrates the potential for increased safety and robustness in practical deployments.

In summary, the results consistently indicate that the AAC strategy improves the performance of VLA models by adaptively selecting action chunk sizes, leading to higher success rates in diverse manipulation tasks in both simulated and real-world environments, while also enhancing robustness and operational safety.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The proposed Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) strategy contributes a practical and effective method to the field of robotic manipulation, particularly for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

*   **Enhanced Performance and Robustness**: By dynamically adjusting the action chunk size based on action entropy, AAC enables VLA models to achieve a favorable balance between reactivity (responding quickly to new information) and temporal consistency (smooth, continuous execution). This leads to substantial improvements in task success rates across a wide variety of manipulation tasks, including those requiring fine-grained control, long-horizon planning, and adaptability to out-of-distribution scenarios. The observed gains in both simulation and real-world experiments validate its ability to improve overall robot performance.
*   **Practical Inference-Time Adaptation**: A key aspect of AAC is its operation solely at inference time, requiring no additional training or architectural modifications to existing VLA models. This makes it a readily deployable solution that can be integrated with current state-of-the-art VLA backbones (e.g., GR00T, $\pi_{0.5}$) without incurring significant development costs or retraining efforts. This plug-and-play compatibility facilitates its adoption in various robotic systems.
*   **Elimination of Empirical Tuning**: The traditional approach of manually tuning a fixed action chunk size is laborious, time-consuming, and often yields suboptimal results across diverse tasks. AAC eliminates this need by adaptively determining the optimal chunk size dynamically, freeing researchers and practitioners from extensive empirical experimentation and making VLA models more scalable and user-friendly.
*   **Improved Safety and Accuracy**: The qualitative results from real-world experiments demonstrate that AAC can lead to more accurate actions and enhance robot safety by preventing collisions. By reducing chunk sizes during critical manipulation phases where uncertainty is high, the robot can execute more precise movements and avoid errors that could result in damage to itself or its environment.
*   **Generalizability Across Task Phases and Robot Embodiments**: The adaptive pattern of AAC, which mirrors human intuition (large chunks for transport, small for critical manipulation), demonstrates its ability to align with the semantic phases of a task. Furthermore, its formulation for action entropy, accommodating both continuous and discrete control, suggests generalizability across different robot embodiments (e.g., single-arm, bimanual, humanoid configurations).
*   **Advancement in VLA Model Deployment**: This work addresses a fundamental limitation in the deployment of VLA models. By offering a robust solution for adaptive action chunking, it contributes to making these models more reliable, efficient, and capable in real-world applications, paving the way for more autonomous and versatile robotic systems.
overview:
## Adaptive Action Chunking for Robotic Control

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful framework for robotic manipulation, enabling robots to interpret complex language instructions and visual scenes to generate precise physical actions. These models, often built upon large-scale vision-language backbones and diffusion-based action heads, typically output a sequence of future actions known as an "action chunk." This paradigm, called action chunking, allows the robot to execute multiple steps without constant replanning, which has been shown to improve temporal consistency and reduce compounding errors.

![Overview of Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) method compared to fixed-size chunking.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/method_1108.png)
*Figure 1: The Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) framework dynamically selects the optimal number of actions to execute from a predicted chunk by evaluating action entropy, whereas standard methods use a fixed length.*

However, a fundamental limitation in current VLA deployments is the reliance on a fixed chunk size during inference. If the chunk size is too large, the robot loses its ability to react to sudden environmental changes or fine-grained corrections needed during delicate tasks like grasping. Conversely, if the chunk size is too small, the robot may exhibit jerky, stuttered movements—a phenomenon known as "mode-jumping"—and suffer from reduced computational efficiency due to frequent, redundant replanning. This research introduces Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC), an inference-time strategy that dynamically adjusts the chunk size based on the model's own predictive uncertainty, eliminating the need for task-specific manual tuning.

## The Trade-off in Action Chunking

In the current landscape of robotic foundation models, such as GR00T or OpenVLA, the choice of the action chunk length $h$ is typically an empirical decision made during the design phase. For example, a common default might be $h = 16$. While this works reasonably well on average, empirical evidence shows that the "optimal" chunk size is highly task-dependent and varies even within a single execution.

Consider a task where a robot must reach across a counter to pick up a mug. During the initial "reaching" phase, the robot is moving through free space where precision is less critical than speed and consistency; here, a large chunk size (e.g., $h = 16$) is beneficial. However, as the gripper approaches the mug handle, the robot enters a "critical manipulation" phase. In this stage, even a few millimeters of error can lead to a collision or a failed grasp. A smaller chunk size (e.g., $h = 4$) would allow for more frequent visual feedback and tighter control loops, significantly increasing the probability of success.

![Task success rates for different fixed chunk sizes across various RoboCasa kitchen tasks.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/PnP_diversity_bestpoints_task_name_2.png)
*Figure 2: Experimental results showing that no single fixed chunk size is optimal for all tasks. Some tasks peak at $h=4$, while others require $h=16$ for maximum performance.*

As shown in Figure 2, the optimal chunk size fluctuates significantly across different kitchen tasks. The inability of static models to adapt to these varying requirements limits their robustness in diverse, unconstrained environments. Prior attempts to solve this involved training separate reinforcement learning agents to choose chunk sizes or using heuristic search strategies, but these often require additional training data or task-specific reward functions that are difficult to obtain in the real world.

## Quantifying Uncertainty via Action Entropy

The core innovation of the Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) method is the use of action entropy as a proxy for predictive confidence. The intuition is straightforward: if the model is confident in its future trajectory, the predicted actions will be consistent across multiple samples, resulting in low entropy. If the model is uncertain—perhaps due to a complex visual scene or an ambiguous task stage—the predicted actions will vary widely, leading to high entropy.

To implement this at inference time without modifying the underlying model, AAC leverages the probabilistic nature of diffusion-based action heads. By sampling $N$ candidate action chunks in parallel, the algorithm can estimate the distribution of predicted actions at each future timestep. The action space typically includes continuous components (translation and rotation) and discrete components (gripper state).

For continuous actions like translation (3-DOF), the method calculates the Gaussian differential entropy. If we represent the estimated covariance matrix of the sampled actions as $\Sigma_t$, the translation entropy $\mathcal{H}_{trans}$ is:

$$\mathcal{H}_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} \ln((2\pi e)^3 |\Sigma_{trans}|)$$

For the discrete gripper state, where $p(a)$ is the probability of the gripper being open or closed based on the $N$ samples, the standard discrete entropy formula is used:

$$\mathcal{H}_{grip} = - \sum_{a \in \{0, 1\}} p(a) \log p(a)$$

The total average action entropy for a potential chunk size $h$ is then calculated by summing the individual entropies (translation, rotation, and gripper) across all timesteps in the chunk and averaging them:

$$E_h = \frac{1}{h} \sum_{i=1}^{h} (\mathcal{H}_{trans,i} + \mathcal{H}_{rot,i} + \mathcal{H}_{grip,i})$$

By monitoring how this average entropy $E_h$ changes as the chunk size increases, the system can identify the point where the model's confidence begins to drop significantly.

## Determining the Optimal Chunk Size

The AAC algorithm determines the optimal chunk size $h^*$ by finding the maximum differential in the average action entropy. Specifically, it looks for the value of $h$ where the jump in uncertainty between $h$ and $h+1$ is the largest. This point represents the boundary of the "reliable" prediction horizon.

To ensure physical consistency and prevent the robot from stuttering with extremely small movements, a lower bound $\xi$ is introduced. This constraint ensures that the executed chunk meets a minimum magnitude of physical movement (in terms of distance or rotation). The final decision rule for the chunk size is:

$$h^* = \max(\arg\max_{h \in \{1, \dots, H-1\}} (E_{h+1} - E_h), \xi)$$

where $H$ is the maximum possible chunk length (the model's prediction horizon). This mathematical framework allows the robot to "know what it doesn't know." When the environment is clear and the goal is certain, the entropy differential remains low for a long time, leading to a large $h^*$. In cluttered or high-precision scenarios, the entropy spikes early, forcing a smaller $h^*$ and more frequent replanning.

![Visualization of adaptive chunk size decisions across different task stages.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/chunk_size_decision.png)
*Figure 3: Semantic alignment of AAC decisions. The model chooses larger chunks ($h^*=14$) for transportation and smaller chunks ($h^*=4$) for fine approach and alignment.*

This behavior is clearly visible in Figure 3. During the initial movement towards a bowl, the robot uses large chunks. As it reaches the "Fine approach" stage, the chunk size drops to 4, allowing the robot to react to the visual feedback of the bowl's edge. Once the object is grasped and the robot moves to place it, the chunk size increases again.

## Simulation and Real-World Performance

The researchers validated AAC across several benchmark suites, including RoboCasa and LIBERO, as well as in real-world physical experiments. In simulation, AAC consistently outperformed vanilla VLA models using fixed chunk sizes. On the RoboCasa Kitchen benchmark, AAC improved the average success rate from 59.7% to 62.0%, with even more significant gains in tasks involving rotation (e.g., turning knobs), which require high precision.

![Distribution of chunk sizes over the course of a simulation task.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/simstep_libero_spatial_task_0.png)
*Figure 4: A heatmap of chunk size decisions over time. The red line represents the mean chunk size, showing how the system dynamically adjusts the execution horizon throughout the task.*

The real-world experiments conducted on a Realman robotic arm demonstrated even more pronounced benefits. In a "Banana Pick-and-Place" task, the success rate jumped from 70% to 90%. Qualitative analysis revealed that the baseline model often failed because a large fixed chunk size caused the gripper to overshoot the target or misalign the orientation before the next replanning cycle could correct it. AAC avoided these issues by automatically reducing the chunk size as the gripper approached the banana.

![Real-world experimental setup for banana picking, button pressing, and drawer manipulation.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/real_world_tasks_2.png)
*Figure 5: The three primary real-world tasks used to evaluate AAC's performance in varied manipulation scenarios.*

Furthermore, AAC showed improved robustness in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. When the positions of objects were perturbed beyond what was seen in the training data, the adaptive mechanism detected the resulting increase in model uncertainty and naturally shifted to smaller, more cautious chunk sizes. This "self-braking" behavior is a critical safety feature for deploying VLA models in unpredictable human environments.

| Task | Baseline ($h=16$) Success | AAC Success |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Banana Pick-and-Place | 70% | 90% |
| E-Button Pressing | 65% | 75% |
| Toy-in-Drawer (Long Horizon) | 65% | 80% |

## Efficiency and Scalability

A common concern with sampling-based methods is the computational overhead. AAC requires generating $N$ candidate chunks (the paper uses $N=20$) to estimate entropy. However, because these chunks can be generated in a single parallel batch on modern GPUs (like the NVIDIA A800), the added latency is minimal—approximately 20 milliseconds per inference step. This is a negligible trade-off considering the substantial gains in success rate and safety.

Moreover, AAC is model-agnostic. The authors demonstrated its effectiveness not only on the GR00T backbone but also on the $\pi_{0.5}$ model, showing consistent improvements across different architectures. This suggests that adaptive chunking could become a standard component of any diffusion-based robotic control pipeline.

![A side-by-side comparison of a failed grasp by the baseline and a successful grasp by AAC.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/banana_baseline.png)
*Figure 6: A baseline failure case where a fixed chunk size leads to a misaligned grasp.*

![Successful grasp using AAC.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04161v1/banana_ours.png)
*Figure 7: The same task successfully completed by AAC, which reduced chunk size for precise alignment.*

## Significance of the Work

The introduction of Adaptive Action Chunking represents a move toward more "intelligent" execution strategies for robotic foundation models. By allowing the model to modulate its own control frequency based on its internal state of uncertainty, AAC bridges the gap between high-level reasoning and low-level reactive control.

The impact of this work is three-fold:
1.  **Practicality**: It provides a "plug-and-play" solution that works at inference time without needing task-specific labels, reward functions, or retraining.
2.  **Performance**: It effectively resolves the long-standing dilemma between reactivity and consistency, leading to higher success rates in complex manipulation.
3.  **Safety**: By detecting high-entropy states, the system can automatically slow down and take smaller steps in uncertain situations, preventing catastrophic failures or collisions.

As VLA models continue to scale and enter more complex, real-world domains, dynamic strategies like AAC will be essential for ensuring that these models can operate reliably across the vast diversity of tasks they will encounter.

keyInsights: Action entropy, derived from parallel sampling of predicted actions, provides a robust and model-agnostic indicator of a VLA model's predictive uncertainty for both continuous and discrete control outputs.

Dynamically adjusting action chunk sizes based on this uncertainty enables VLA models to adapt their control granularity to specific task phases, leveraging larger chunks for confident, coarse movements and smaller ones for critical, fine-grained manipulations.

The point of maximum differential in average action entropy, combined with a minimum action magnitude constraint, effectively determines an optimal chunk size at inference time, balancing temporal consistency with reactivity to environmental changes.
originalProblem: Fixed action chunk sizes in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models necessitate a trade-off between reactivity to new environmental information (requiring small chunks) and temporal consistency/computational efficiency (benefiting from large chunks).

The optimal action chunk size varies considerably across different robotic manipulation tasks and even within distinct phases of a single task, making manual empirical tuning challenging and inherently suboptimal.

Previous adaptive chunking approaches often rely on hand-crafted value functions, require task-specific reward signals unavailable in real-world scenarios, or do not offer true dynamic adaptation, limiting their generalization and practical deployment.
results: In simulation, AAC improved average success rates by 2.3% on RoboCasa Kitchen (62.0% vs 59.7%) and 0.9% on LIBERO (95.0% vs 94.1%), demonstrating consistent gains across diverse benchmarks and notably a 4% increase in LIBERO-Long tasks.

Real-world experiments on a robot showed a 15% average increase in success rate (from 67.0% to 82.0%) across banana pick-and-place, emergency button pressing, and long-horizon toy manipulation tasks, attributed to better gripper alignment and precision.

AAC enhanced robustness in out-of-distribution scenarios and improved operational safety in real-world deployments by preventing collisions and enabling smoother, more precise actions when predictions indicated high uncertainty.
solution: Introduces Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC), an inference-time strategy that dynamically selects the optimal action chunk size based on the VLA model's predictive uncertainty, without requiring additional training or architectural modifications.

Utilizes action entropy as a metric to quantify the uncertainty of predicted actions; lower entropy suggests higher reliability for longer action chunks, while higher entropy indicates uncertain predictions warranting smaller chunk sizes and more frequent replanning.

Calculates average action entropy by sampling multiple candidate action chunks in parallel, using Gaussian differential entropy for continuous control components (translation, rotation) and standard discrete entropy for discrete components (gripper), then identifies the optimal chunk size where the increase in average entropy is maximal, constrained by a minimum action magnitude.
summary.summary: A new inference-time strategy, Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC), allows Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to dynamically determine action chunk sizes based on predictive uncertainty. This method enhances task success rates on RoboCasa Kitchen and LIBERO benchmarks in simulation, achieving an average 2.3% and 0.9% increase respectively, and improves real-world robot manipulation by 15% across several tasks.

title: Adaptive Action Chunking at Inference-time for Vision-Language-Action Models
